text:
A rocky shore. SERAPHEL, exiled Queen of Valdris, stands apart. Her spirit LUMEN attends her.
SERAPHEL. They pass me by unknowing — eyes made blind By guilt and sea-salt both. Go, Lumen, fetch My crown and robes from out the cave. [Exit Lumen.] I'll shed this beggar's guise and stand once more As once I stood in Valdris. Swift, good spirit; Thy service nears its end.
Lumen re-enters, singing, and bears the robes.
LUMEN. Where the wind turns, there turn I, Through the hollow reeds I sigh; Where the river meets the sea, There shall I at last run free. Merrily, merrily shall I fly Past the sun and past the sky.
SERAPHEL. My faithful Lumen! Thou shalt have thy freedom; But first, one final office: go unseen Unto the vessel anchored in the bay, And wake the captain from his enchant'd sleep. Bid him attend us here.
LUMEN. I move like thought, and am returned Before the tide can turn a stone. [Exit.]
DUKE ALDRIC. What strangeness holds this coast? Some power bends The air about us. Heaven, be our guide!
SERAPHEL. Look well upon me, brother. I am she Whom you displaced — Seraphel, rightful queen. No phantom this: reach out and take my hand, And feel the warmth of living blood beneath.
ALDRIC. Thou canst not be — thou wert three years at sea, Or drowned — thy pulse — it beats — what sorcery Hath kept thee? Or do I, with fever'd mind, Conjure the face I wronged?
SERAPHEL. No conjuring. The isle hath sheltered me, and taught me patience. That crown which you usurped — I do require it, Though not in malice ask I its return; For I have found, in exile's bitter school, That power without pardon is a prison.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] My conscience wrings me. She was ever just.
SERAPHEL. [Aside to Montarvo.] Thou too wast party to it. I have known These three years past. Yet look, I hold no blade Above thee: thou shalt have thy peace from me If peace is what thou seek'st.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] The devil's mercy — no, it is a saint's.
ALDRIC. If thou art truly she, then tell me how Thou cam'st to live? We watched the tempest break Thy ship to timber. I — I grieve that sight More than thou know'st. My crown sat heavy on me Each morning after.
SERAPHEL. I believe thee, Aldric; And grief is a beginning. I was cast Upon this shore, and found it strange and kind. I shall not now rehearse these years entire — The tale would swallow daylight whole. Know this: I lived, I learned, I lost things dear to me As thou hast too, I warrant. We are both Made lesser by these years, and yet more whole. Take back my hand. This rocky court hath been My throne-room long enough. Let us go home.

A rocky shore. SERAPHEL, exiled Queen of Valdris, stands apart. Her spirit LUMEN attends her.
SERAPHEL. They walk straight past me. Three years gone, and not one eye Knows mine. Go, Lumen — fetch my robes and crown From the cave. [Exit Lumen.] I'll put this beggar's skin Behind me, stand as I was. Be quick now; Thy service is nearly done.
Lumen re-enters bearing the robes, singing.
LUMEN. Where the wind turns, there turn I, Through the hollow reeds I sigh; Where the river meets the sea, There shall I at last run free. Merrily, merrily shall I fly Past the sun and past the sky.
SERAPHEL. Good Lumen. Thou shalt have thy liberty soon enough — But one last errand: go silent to the bay, Find the captain of that vessel sleeping Beneath enchantment still, and wake him. Send him to me here.
LUMEN. I am gone and back before The tide shifts a pebble. [Exit.]
DUKE ALDRIC. What place is this? The very air feels bent. Some power dwells here. God guide us out of it.
SERAPHEL. Look at me properly, Aldric. It is I — Seraphel. The queen you set aside. No spirit, no trick of the light — give me your hand. You feel that? That is blood, and it is warm.
ALDRIC. This cannot — thou wert lost at sea, three years, We saw the ship — thy pulse does beat — I am Not well, I think — some fever holds me still —
SERAPHEL. No fever. The isle kept me. I kept myself. And now I want what's mine returned — not seized By rage, not out of fury. I have had Three years to learn what fury costs, and it costs much. I ask it plainly: give me back my crown.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] She was always just. God, she was always just.
SERAPHEL. [Aside to Montarvo.] I know thy part in it. Have known these three years. Yet I've no blade for thee — only this offer: Come honestly before me and we're done with it.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] This is either mercy or it's witchcraft. And I think it mercy.
ALDRIC. If thou art truly living, tell me how. We watched the storm tear that ship into kindling — I have not — there is not a morning since I have not thought of it. The crown has been No easy thing to wear, I swear to thee.
SERAPHEL. I believe thee. Grief is something, Aldric — It is not nothing. I was thrown ashore here Half-dead, and the isle was strange, and then less strange. I will not tell the whole of it today; The story is three years long and the light Is going. Know only this: I lived. I lost things, Same as thee. We are both worn by it — Perhaps that is sufficient ground to start from. Take my hand again. This shore has served As court to me too long. Let us go home.

Now having the better part of two days to pass in Gloucester before the coastal packet would take me further north, the immediate question pressing upon me was where I should find a bed and a meal at a price my circumstances could honestly sustain. The night was raw and wet, the kind that doesn't announce itself with any honest downpour but simply saturates everything by slow degrees, until a man finds his collar soaked without ever having felt a drop fall. I knew nobody in Gloucester. My funds, such as they were, I had already reckoned twice over in my coat pocket as I walked, and the sum did not improve with repetition. Well then, said I to myself, stopping beneath the overhang of a chandler's shop and watching the rain bead on the cobblestones, the matter is simple enough: you want a roof, Nathaniel, and four walls, and something warm in a bowl, and you want them cheap. Pride has no lodgings to offer you tonight.
I made my way along the main street first, as a man will do who knows he cannot afford the main street. The Anchor and Crown had lamplight spilling handsomely from every window and the sound of fiddle-playing within—too handsome entirely, and I moved along. Further down stood the Mariner's Rest, whose sign depicted a sailor in a hammock of improbable comfort, and from whose open door proceeded a smell of roasting meat so persuasive I had to address myself sternly and keep walking. These establishments were for men with warmer pockets than mine. The cobblestones turned uneven underfoot where the frost had buckled them, and my left boot, whose sole had been making silent but unmistakable complaints for a fortnight, now began to admit the wet.
I turned instinctively toward the water, reasoning as I had always reasoned in such ports, that cheapness follows the smell of brine. The streets narrowed. The lamplight thinned to almost nothing. Here were buildings that leaned toward one another overhead as if sharing confidences, and underfoot the ground was uncertain—rope-ends, a broken oar, the dark slick of fish-scales on stone. On one corner I found what I took for an inn, pushed open the door, and discovered instead a room full of men engaged in some sort of dispute over a net, who looked up at me without welcome and then looked away again without curiosity. I withdrew.
At last, near the waterfront proper, I came upon a narrow building wedged between a rope-walk and a warehouse, with a board hanging above its door on a rusted chain. The board bore, or had once borne, some sort of painted device—a fish, perhaps, or an anchor—but the weather had long since arbitrated the question in favour of blankness. Beneath it, in letters still just legible: Harmon's—Beds and Supper. No flourishes. No promises of comfort. The building itself listed slightly to one side, as though decades of Atlantic wind had been pushing at it from the northwest and it had simply accommodated itself to the pressure. The single window showed a light so faint it might have been a candle guttering in a back room, or a man's pipe-glow, or merely the memory of light. It had the look, altogether, of a place that expected nothing from you and therefore offered honest value—which is to say, it had the look of the very place I wanted. The door, when I tried it, opened without ceremony.

I had the better part of two days to kill in Gloucester before the coastal packet would carry me north, which meant finding a bed and a meal at a price I could actually meet. The night was raw — not a proper rain, just the kind of wet that works its way in gradually, so you're soaked before you've noticed a single drop. I knew no one in town. I'd counted my money twice already, walking, and it hadn't improved either time. All right then, I said to myself, ducking under a chandler's awning to watch the rain bead on the cobbles — the situation is plain enough. You need a roof, Nathaniel. Four walls, something hot, and the whole affair cheap. This is not the night for dignity.
The main street I walked as a kind of penance, knowing what I'd find. The Anchor and Crown had lamplight in every window and a fiddle going inside — too warm, too bright, no question. The Mariner's Rest stood further along, its sign showing a sailor in a hammock that no hammock had ever resembled, and from the open door came roasting meat on the air, persistent and persuasive. I talked myself past it. My left boot, which had been suggesting for some weeks that its useful life was behind it, now settled the matter and let the wet in.
I turned toward the water. It had always been sound logic in ports like this — cheapness and brine go together. The streets narrowed fast. The lamps gave out. Buildings leaned at each other overhead like men at a bar, and underfoot there was rope, a broken oar, the black slickness of old fish-scales on stone. I pushed open what I took for an inn on one corner, and found instead a roomful of men in heated argument over a net. They looked up, registered nothing of interest, and returned to their quarrel. I stepped back out.
Then, hard by the waterfront, wedged between a rope-walk and a warehouse as though it had grown there by accident, there was a narrow building with a board swinging on a rusted chain above the door. Whatever had once been painted on it — fish, anchor, who could say — the weather had long since made a blank of it. Beneath, in letters you could still just read: Harmon's — Beds and Supper. No embellishment. The building itself leaned slightly into the prevailing wind, the way old things do when they've simply stopped resisting. One window showed a light that might have been a candle in a back room, or a pipe-glow, or the idea of light rather than the thing itself. It had exactly the look of a place that expected nothing from you and therefore asked nothing beyond what was fair — which, on a night like that one, was recommendation enough. The door opened easily, without drama.

Drums and alarums. KING MALDREN enters with soldiers. His mother, LADY EDWYNN, intercepts him. LADY CORNYS, widow of Lord Hest, stands apart.
LADY EDWYNN. Stay, son, and hear me.
KING MALDREN. Mother, step aside. The field will not be kept by women's tongues. Beat drum!
LADY EDWYNN. Beat drum and thou wilt drown my words, But not my grief, which is too deep for drowning.
KING MALDREN. I am in haste.
LADY EDWYNN. Ay, ever hast thou been. In haste to enter on this wretched world And fill it with thy purposes and ruin. Thou wert not born, but launched, as men launch stone From engines built for breaking—
KING MALDREN. Peace, good madam.
LADY EDWYNN. Good? What goodness hast thou left in thee That I should scruple to withhold my curse? Thy boyhood was a catalogue of harms, Thy youth a sharper chapter, and thine age Is the whole book bound up in blood and burning. What gentle hour, what moment of thy life Hath earned thee one soft prayer from out my lips?
KING MALDREN. You nursed me once. Claim what is owed on that.
LADY EDWYNN. The breast that fed thee pays its debt in sorrow. Go then, thou, and carry hence my curse As thou wouldst carry armour to the field— It shall weigh heavier than steel, and slower Drag thee toward thy end. The prayers I offer Fly to the other side, and there take arms Against thee with the men thou goest to meet. Those souls that loved thee once and found thee false Cry out before the throne for thy destruction. Go, Maldren. Thou art built of harm, and harm Shall be the house wherein thy life is ended. [Exit.]
LADY CORNYS. She speaks for all the dead and half the living. I have not her years nor yet her fury, But every word she spoke, I seal with mine.
KING MALDREN. Lady, stay. There is a matter between us.
LADY CORNYS. There is no matter left. My lord is gone. My sons are gone. What matter couldst thou raise That grief hath not already swallowed whole?
KING MALDREN. You have a daughter, fair and worthy, Cornys.
LADY CORNYS. And wouldst thou make her instrument of peace? Then take her not — I'll mar what thou wouldst use; I'll say she was not Hest's but gotten basely, I'll clothe her beauty in such slander's rags No crown could ever find occasion for her.
KING MALDREN. Wrong not the girl; her blood is good and known.
LADY CORNYS. That very blood turned poison in my sons, And they were of that selfsame lineage. Their birth was noble — see what good it brought them.
KING MALDREN. Their stars were crossed at birth; 'twas destiny.
LADY CORNYS. 'Twas thou. Dress murder how thou wilt in stars, The hand that loosed the bolt was thine in theirs. No fate decreed it but the will of one Who wore the outward show of kinsman's love And inward harboured nothing but his own. Thou talkest destiny to stop my mouth As thou didst stop theirs — quietly, and early, Before they grew enough to speak against thee. But I am grown, and grief hath made me bold, And I will speak though every drum thou hast Beat on until they split.
KING MALDREN. Madam, as I hope To prosper in this war and come home crowned, My intentions toward your daughter and yourself Are kinder far than all you have yet known!

Drums and alarums. KING MALDREN enters with soldiers. His mother, LADY EDWYNN, intercepts him. LADY CORNYS, widow of Lord Hest, stands apart.
LADY EDWYNN. Stay, son. I will be heard.
KING MALDREN. Stand clear, good mother. The field waits on no woman's grief. Beat drum!
LADY EDWYNN. Beat every drum thou hast — drown out my words. The grief beneath them is too deep for that.
KING MALDREN. I cannot stop. Not now.
LADY EDWYNN. No, never stopped — Not from the hour thou first thrust into the world To wrench and break whatever lay in reach. Thou wert not born, but hurled, as men hurl stone From engines made for nothing else but ruin.
KING MALDREN. Enough, madam.
LADY EDWYNN. Enough? Name me one hour — One gentle hour, one moment of thy life — That ever earned a soft word from my lips. Thy boyhood was a ledger full of harm. Thy youth a sharper version of the same. And now thine age is every page together, Bound hard in blood.
KING MALDREN. You suckled me. Take comfort in that debt.
LADY EDWYNN. The breast that fed thee pays it back in sorrow. Go, then, and carry what I give thee now— A mother's curse—as thou wouldst carry armour: It will sit heavier than steel, trust that. My prayers ride with thine enemies tonight. The souls thou wronged are there before the throne Crying against thee. Maldren — thou art built of harm. And harm shall be the room wherein thou diest. [Exit.]
LADY CORNYS. She speaks for the dead and half the living both. I lack her years, and she outstrips me in fury, But every word she said — I say it too.
KING MALDREN. A moment, lady. There is business between us.
LADY CORNYS. There's nothing left. My lord is gone. My sons Are gone. What business could be left to do That grief hasn't already had its way with?
KING MALDREN. Your daughter — she is fair, well-born, worth much.
LADY CORNYS. Wouldst use her as a clasp to pin things shut? Then I'll undo the clasp before thou reach it — I'll call her baseborn, gotten out of wedlock, Dress her in slander till no court could want her.
KING MALDREN. Wrong not the girl; her blood is beyond question.
LADY CORNYS. That blood ran in my sons. I know its worth. See what their noble birth afforded them.
KING MALDREN. Their stars were set against them. Destiny.
LADY CORNYS. Thy hands were set against them. Call it that. No constellation killed them — thou didst, quietly, Before they found their voices to speak back. But mine is found, and grief has made it hard, And I will use it though thy every drum Beats on until the skins split through.
KING MALDREN. Lady, as I Would prosper in this war and see it ended — I mean your daughter and yourself more good Than all the harm that's fallen on you yet!

"Civil words!" she cried, turning on her husband with a look that would have felled a less composed man than myself to witness. "If you had not the spirit to stand your ground, you should have remained in your study and saved us all the performance. It is precisely what I expected—precisely! You will persist in reading weakness as virtue and call it patience, and then wonder that the world treats you as it does. No, I shall not open it—not for you, not for anyone! I have spent the better part of a year smoothing over your quarrels and flattering your vanity, and this is my reward—two fools united in blaming the only person in the house who possesses an ounce of sense!"
Frederick grew very white. He was not a man who managed rage with any dignity; it drained out of him and left something worse in its place, a kind of cold, shaking helplessness that I always found more alarming than an outburst. He set his hand on the doorframe and said nothing for a moment, and Marian watched him with her chin raised and her eyes bright with something between contempt and a wild satisfaction.
"Your husband," said Ravenswood, with a short laugh, moving from his position by the window, "appears to be rehearsing his death-scene. Shall I applaud, or wait for the interval?" He looked at her as he said it rather than at Frederick, which told me everything I needed to know about the state of matters between the three of them.
What happened next I could not have predicted, though perhaps I should have. Frederick, who had seemed on the verge of some silent collapse, crossed the room in three steps and struck Ravenswood across the jaw with a force that surprised us all, Ravenswood included. He staggered back against the sideboard; the decanter went over, and glass spread across the floorboards in a bright fan. For a moment nobody moved.
"Get out," said Marian—not to Ravenswood, but to her husband. Her voice had gone flat and strange.
Frederick walked out. We heard his feet on the stairs, steady and deliberate.
Ravenswood straightened, touched his jaw, and said nothing. His eyes had gone very dark. I knew that look, and I wanted no part of what it promised.
"He'll have gone for Dawson and the groom," I said quickly, which was not entirely a falsehood, for it seemed probable enough. "There are three of them at least, and you would do well to be gone before they find you here with a smashed decanter and a reason to use it against you."
Whether he believed me or not, he went—through the garden door, without ceremony or farewell.
Marian sat down heavily in the armchair and pressed both hands to her temples. "Lock that door, Joanna," she said, without looking at me. "And if you find Leonard before I do, tell him I am unwell and not to be disturbed tonight." She paused. "I am not unwell, you understand. But I intend to be, if it comes to it. He will come up here with his grievances all in order and his feelings freshly sharpened, and I cannot answer for myself if he does. Tell him I am sleeping. Tell him I was taken faint. Tell him whatever will keep him at the other end of the corridor until I have had an hour to think." She looked up at me then, and the brightness had gone out of her face, leaving something raw and exhausted underneath. "You don't think it my fault, Joanna? You saw how it began—you know I had no intention of any of this."

"Civil words!" she turned on her husband with a look that would have felled a steadier man than I to witness. "If you'd had the spine to stand your ground, you might have stayed in your study and spared us the performance. Exactly as I expected—exactly! You call your weakness patience and wonder why nobody respects it. No—I shan't open it. Not for you." She pressed back against the door with a kind of fierce pleasure in her own obstruction. "A year I've spent smoothing your quarrels and flattering your pride, and here is what it comes to—two fools in perfect agreement, blaming the one person in the house with any sense!"
Frederick went very white. He had never managed rage gracefully; it always drained out of him and left something worse behind—a cold, shaking helplessness I found more alarming than any outburst. He set one hand on the doorframe and said nothing, and Marian watched him with her chin up and something in her eyes that was half contempt and half a wild, unsettling pleasure.
"Your husband," said Ravenswood from his place by the window, with a short laugh, "appears to be rehearsing his death-scene." He looked at her as he said it, not at Frederick. That told me everything about the three of them I had not already understood.
What came next I could not have anticipated. Frederick—who had seemed to be folding inward toward some silent collapse—crossed the room and struck Ravenswood across the jaw with a force that surprised us all, Ravenswood particularly. He went back hard against the sideboard; the decanter tipped and shattered, glass fanning bright across the floorboards. Nobody moved.
"Go," said Marian. Not to Ravenswood. To her husband. Her voice had gone flat and strange.
Frederick went. His feet on the stairs were steady and deliberate, which was almost worse than if he'd run.
Ravenswood straightened and touched his jaw. His eyes had gone very dark and I knew that look, and I wanted nothing to do with what it was promising.
"He'll have gone for Dawson and the groom," I said quickly—not entirely a lie, it seemed probable enough. "Three of them at least. You'd do well to be elsewhere before they find you standing over a broken decanter."
He went, through the garden door, without looking back.
Marian dropped into the armchair and pressed both hands to her temples. "Lock that door, Joanna. And if you find Leonard, tell him I'm unwell and not to be disturbed." A pause. "I'm not unwell—not yet. But I shall be, if it comes to it. He'll arrive with his grievances all set in order and his feelings freshly honed, and I cannot answer for what I'll say. Tell him I was taken faint. Tell him I'm sleeping. Whatever keeps him at the other end of the corridor until I've had an hour." She looked up at me then, and the brightness had gone out entirely, leaving something raw underneath. "You don't think it my fault, Joanna? You were here from the beginning—you saw how it started."

CAVENDISH. For this at least: that we who draw our swords Do so in a cause our hearts can answer for. Come what may after.
Enter a Soldier, breathless.
SOLDIER. My lord, make ready. Lord Pemberton advances with his van.
CAVENDISH. Good. He spares me the concluding of a speech I had no art to finish. Mark me, friends: Words are the trade of peacetime. Here is mine.
He draws.
Let every man acquit himself as well As his worst enemy would fear he might. Strike hard. Strike clean. And if we do not meet Again in such a fellowship as this— Why, we have met today, and that must serve.
Trumpets. They embrace and exeunt.

The field between the armies. Alarums. Enter LORD FENWICK and Sir THOMAS CRAY.
CRAY. Who are you, sir, that dog me through this field As though my death were all your business here?
FENWICK. My name is Fenwick. I pursue thee, Cray, Because they point at thee and say: the King.
CRAY. They point at truth.
FENWICK. Then truth shall serve my sword As well today as falsehood ever did. The Earl of Mortham fell not half an hour past, Thinking he struck at thee. I'll not mistake. Yield, or the field shall have thee.
CRAY. Not from me. I have not lived a king to die a prisoner.
They fight. Cray is slain. Enter Cavendish.
CAVENDISH. Well struck, my lord. Is't done?
FENWICK. Aye, here he lies. The King himself, at last.
CAVENDISH. The King? Look well. I know that face, and it is not the King's. This is Sir Thomas Cray — a valiant man, Accoutred so like royalty that God Himself might pause before He told the difference.
FENWICK. They dressed him like a king and sent him out To draw our swords and spend our time upon him?
CAVENDISH. The King keeps many images in the field. Strike one and two more rise.
FENWICK. Then I shall strike Each one in turn until the true face shows. I'll work my way through every coat he owns.
CAVENDISH. Work quickly then. Our line still holds, but barely.
Exeunt. Alarums. Enter GREYWICK alone.
GREYWICK. I'll confess it freely: I am not well. I have been in three places on this field today where sensible men were not. My company— God rest what's left of it—are scattered to the hedgerows and the ditches hereabouts, finding religion, no doubt, in the mud. I myself have done extraordinary things. Extraordinary. I shall remember them when I am far from here and safe.
He sees a body.
Ah. Sir Thomas Cray. There's a lesson in thee, friend. Thou put the King's own colours on thy back, and see what honour bought thee — a quiet lie in a field that nobody will name correctly in any ballad sung a year from now. Honour! I have thought the matter over at some length today, and mostly from positions of considerable personal discomfort, and I find it a commodity best viewed from the outside, at a prudent distance, like a fire that warms the room but need not be embraced directly.
Enter Lord Pemberton's son, young PEMBERTON.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Greywick! Why stand'st thou here? Give me thy sword — I've snapped mine at the hilt. Three of Lord Fenwick's captains yet are loose and doing damage we shall all regret. Thy sword, man — quickly!
GREYWICK. My sword is — I am — the blade has a particular stiffness today that I cannot account for.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Give it me!
GREYWICK. Here then — though I warn thee it has grown unaccustomed to the work.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. What's this? This is a — is this a flask?
GREYWICK. It is medicinal. The case looked similar. An easy error. Any man might—
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Is there a battle in which thou art not ridiculous?
He hurls it at Greywick and exits.
GREYWICK. If Fenwick still breathes, I shall find him — possibly. If he finds me first, I shall endure it nobly. There is a kind of honour even in surviving, though I grant you no one writes songs about it. Fortune, thou art a woman, and as such I have always found thee best approached with patience, flattery, and modest expectations.
Exit.

Another part of the field. Alarums. Enter KING EDWARD, young PEMBERTON, and LORD MARSHAL.
KING. Pemberton, thou'rt hurt — fall back, I prithee. Lord Marshal, see him clear of this press.
LORD MARSHAL. Not while I stand, my liege, unless I bleed as freely as he does.

CAVENDISH. This much at least: that we who lift our swords do so with hearts that will not disown the cause. Whatever follows.
Enter a Soldier, breathless.
SOLDIER. My lord — be ready. Lord Pemberton's van is moving. Now.
CAVENDISH. Good. He saves me from a speech I had no ending for. Here, then, is all of it.
He draws.
Let every man do what his worst enemy would dread to find him capable of. Strike well. And if this fellowship should not assemble twice — it has assembled once. That is not nothing. On.
Trumpets. They embrace and exexit.

The open field. Alarums. Enter LORD FENWICK and SIR THOMAS CRAY.
CRAY. Who are you, that you've followed me this far? What is it you want from me?
FENWICK. My name is Fenwick. I follow because they tell me thou art King.
CRAY. They tell thee right.
FENWICK. Then right shall do as well for my sword's purpose as the wrong ever did. The Earl of Mortham fell not half an hour past — thought he was striking thee. I'll not mistake. Yield, or the field has thee.
CRAY. Not from me. I have not been a king to end a prisoner.
They fight. Cray falls. Enter Cavendish.
CAVENDISH. Is it done?
FENWICK. Done. The King himself.
CAVENDISH. Look closer. I know that face. This is Sir Thomas Cray — brave man, well-dressed, not remotely the King. They've turned him out in royal colours to draw our swords off course and waste our afternoon.
FENWICK. A borrowed face. He bought it dear enough.
CAVENDISH. The King runs several copies in the field. Strike one and another rises.
FENWICK. Then I'll go through every coat he owns until I find the living man inside one.
CAVENDISH. Quickly, then.
Exeunt. Enter GREYWICK, alone.
GREYWICK. I'll be honest with myself, at least, here in this quiet corner of an otherwise extremely loud afternoon: I am not well. I have occupied three portions of this field that no man of reasonable disposition should have gone near. My company — such as survived the first half-hour — have redistributed themselves among the ditches and the hedgerows, where, I don't doubt, they are making their peace with God and examining their life's choices. I've done remarkable things today. Remarkable. I shall recall them warmly from a distance.
He notices the body.
Sir Thomas Cray. There's a homily in thee, friend. Dressed in the King's own livery, sent out to make a brave show of it — and here thou art, in a field that no ballad will name correctly for as long as ballads are sung. Honour. I have given honour serious thought today, primarily from positions of acute personal discomfort, and I find it best appreciated from the outside — like a large fire: warming at a distance, inadvisable to embrace directly.
Enter young PEMBERTON.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Greywick — why art thou standing? Give me thy sword. Mine snapped. There are three of Fenwick's captains still loose and doing mischief. Thy sword, man.
GREYWICK. My sword — it's — there is a condition of the blade —
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Give it here!
GREYWICK. There. Though I warn thee it has grown somewhat theoretical about its purpose.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. This is — this is a flask.
GREYWICK. The cases are extremely similar. Any man, in haste —
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Is there a battle in which thou art not a fool?
He throws it at Greywick and exits.
GREYWICK. If Fenwick lives, I'll find him — eventually. If he finds me, I shall bear it with composure. There is an honour in mere continuance that nobody has ever set to music, which I have always thought a gap in the tradition.
Exit.

Another part of the field. Alarums. Enter KING EDWARD, young PEMBERTON, LORD MARSHAL.
KING. Harry — thou bleedest. Fall back, I tell thee. Lord Marshal, take him off the field.
LORD MARSHAL. Not I, my liege — not unless I'm bleeding half as badly.

Their wits thus clouded, and their terrors keen, They stumbled through the thickets wild and green; Each shadow seemed a beast, each branch a hand, And nothing answered as the frightened planned. I drove them deeper, laughing as I flew, And left sweet Cobweb tangled in the dew. So when the sleeper stirred beneath the oak, 'Twas on the strangest face that she first woke.
SILVANUS. Better than craft could compass or contrive! But hast thou touched the other pair with love, As my instruction bid thee from above?
SPRITE. The man I found in sleep beneath the thorn, And marked his eyes before the break of morn; The girl beside him — she, when he shall rise, Must be the very first to meet his eyes.
Enter Celia and Florian.
SILVANUS. Draw close. This is the man we mean to mend.
SPRITE. The girl is right. The man is the wrong friend.
FLORIAN. Why dost thou punish me with such cold scorn, Whose love for thee hath ever faithful born? Reserve such sharpness for an enemy, And turn, I beg thee, softer eyes on me.
CELIA. I chide thee mildly now; I could do worse, For thou hast given me cause enough to curse. If thou hast stolen Silvio from my side Or done him harm while I unknowing lied In sleep beside him — speak! The sun doth keep Less faithful to the day than he to me. That he would leave me, I no sooner trust Than that the sea might turn to summer dust. Thy face confesses it — that hollow look, Like one who knows too well the deed he took.
FLORIAN. That hollow look is grief of thy unkindness, And thou the murderer of my heart's blindness. Yet thou look'st bright as any morning star, As one whose conscience carries not a scar.
CELIA. What do I care for that? Where is he gone? Wilt thou restore him, or shall I mourn alone?
FLORIAN. I'd sooner give his shadow to the wind.
CELIA. Thou hound! Thou faithless, hollow-hearted hind! Hast thou then slain him — tell me, once, speak plain! From this day forth be numbered not with men again! A coward's hand might do the thing awake; Thou did'st it sleeping, for a coward's sake. No serpent struck so basely in the dark, And none had quite so forked a tongue to mark.
FLORIAN. Thou pour'st this anger on a groundless fear. I have not harmed him — know I know not where. Nor can I swear if he be dead or whole.
CELIA. Then swear to me he lives. That much I'll take.
FLORIAN. And what is that worth, given for thy sake?
CELIA. That thou shalt never look upon me more. And so I'll take my leave and shut that door — Alive or dead, he is no care of thine.
Exit.
FLORIAN. Her fury makes all following unwise. I'll rest then here beneath these clouded skies. For grief compounded upon grief doth grow Too heavy for a waking man to know; Sleep is the one poor coin that sorrow spends To lighten debt before the dark night ends.
He lies down.
SILVANUS. What hast thou done? Thou hast undone my plan, And set the love-drops on the wrong-loved man. Of thy misreading must it follow then That true love wanders false and back again.
SPRITE. What wonder? Fate decrees that for each heart Held true, a thousand others fall apart.
SILVANUS. Go sweep the wood more swiftly than the gale, And seek out Rosalind, whose cheeks grow pale With love's own fever — sighing out her days, Her colour spent in unrequited ways. Bring her by seeming here before my art Can plant a new dream in this sleeping heart.
SPRITE. I fly, I fly — no arrow parts the air With half such speed as I go hunting there.
Exit.
SILVANUS. Blossom dipped in sorrow's dye, Struck through by a careless sky, Settle softly in his eye. When his true love passes by, Let her seem to glorify All the stars that light the night — Wake to her and make her right.
Enter Sprite.
SPRITE. Master of our moonlit ring, Rosalind is near — I bring Also him who should not be, Pledging love on bended knee. Shall we stay and watch this scene? Lord, what fools these hearts have been!
SILVANUS. Peace — the noise will rouse the sleeper here.
SPRITE. Then shall two suits clash upon one ear, And a doubled wooing make a single sport. These tangled mortals are the finest sort: Give me a muddle well and truly made, And I am happiest in its motley shade.

Their reason gone, their terrors growing strong, The very branches seemed to do them wrong; Thorns caught at sleeves and briars snagged at hats, And every shadow moved like startled cats. I chased them further in, laughing as I flew, And left poor Cobweb knotted in the dew. So when the sleeper stirred beneath the oak It was the strangest face on which she woke.
SILVANUS. This falls out better than I'd dared to hope. But have you touched the other pair, and set The love-drops on his eyes, as I directed?
SPRITE. Found him sleeping under the thorn-tree — Done and finished. And the girl beside him, So that when he stirs she's the first thing seen.
Enter Celia and Florian.
SILVANUS. Keep still. That is the very man.
SPRITE. The girl's right. The man isn't.
FLORIAN. Why spend your breath so bitterly on me, Who've given you nothing but devotion? Save that for someone who deserves it — turn Those eyes on me for once without the frost.
CELIA. I'm mild now. I could be considerably worse. Thou hast given me reason enough for a curse. If thou hast crept upon Silvio sleeping And done him harm — speak it out. The sun Is not more constant to the day than he Was to me. That he'd slip away from me sleeping — I'd sooner believe the sea would dry to dust. Thou know'st what thou hast done. That face says so.
FLORIAN. This face is grief — thy grief, thy sharpness, thine. And thou the one who keeps on drawing blood And looking clear and bright as a morning star With nothing on thy conscience, apparently.
CELIA. I've no interest in that. Where is he? Wilt thou give him back?
FLORIAN. I'd sooner give his shadow to the wind.
CELIA. Thou dog. Thou faithless hound. Hast killed him then — say it plainly, once — And be among men never more. One word Of truth — was it sleeping that thou did it? A worm might manage that; an adder would. And thou hast an adder's tongue to match the deed — More forked than any serpent in this wood.
FLORIAN. Thou throw'st this fury at a wrong address. I've not touched him — nor do I know where he is — Nor whether he's alive or dead, for that.
CELIA. Then tell me he is living. That will do.
FLORIAN. And what do I get for the telling of it?
CELIA. That thou need never look at me again. And so — whatever hath become of him — I leave thee here with it.
Exit.
FLORIAN. There's no following her when she's like this. I'll stay then. Sorrow heaped on sorrow grows Too much for wakefulness to carry long — sleep is the one coin grief has left to spend before the night runs out. I'll spend a little.
He lies down.
SILVANUS. What hast thou done? Thou hast applied the drops To the one man who needed no amendment — Left true love confused and tangled worse, And done the false love not a bit of harm.
SPRITE. Well — fate seems determined that for every true heart A million others come apart. It's just the way of things.
SILVANUS. Go through the wood — faster than the wind — and find me Helena, who wanders pale and sighing out her colour for a love that hasn't looked at her. Fetch her by seeming — I'll treat his eyes before she comes in sight.
SPRITE. Gone already — no arrow travels faster.
Exit.
SILVANUS. Blossom bruised by careless sky, Let him see through different eyes — when his true love passes by let her be a morning star. Wake and wonder what thou art.
Enter Sprite.
SPRITE. Captain — Helena is nearly here, And the other one I muddled, making vows To whoever comes in range. Do we watch? Lord — there is nothing like a fool in love.
SILVANUS. Quietly — the noise will wake the sleeper.
SPRITE. Two suits pressed at once on the same soul — That is rare sport even by this wood's standards. Give me a tangle properly arranged And I am in my element entirely.

BARDOLPH. Ha! What madness grips thee, thou hollow-cheeked rogue? Dost thou dare stand before me and speak of honour, thou that hast none? Away! The very stench of thy pretence turns my stomach.
EVANS. I do beseech you most earnestly, thou scabrous and pestilent fellow, at my entreaties and my urgings and my solemn requests, to take up and consume, look you, this onion. Because, look you, you do profess to have no love of it, and your humours and your constitution and your digestion do not welcome it, therefore I do most particularly desire you to eat it.
BARDOLPH. Not for Merlin and all his prophecies.
EVANS. There is a prophecy for you. [Strikes him.] Will you be so gracious, thou mangy wretch, as to eat it?
BARDOLPH. Thou wretched cur, I shall have satisfaction.
EVANS. You speak with great confidence, thou festering knave, and so you shall, when Providence ordains it. Until such hour I would have you remain among the living and consume your vittles. Come, here is somewhat to season it. [Strikes him.] You called me yesterday a hill-dwelling pedant; but I will make you today a scholar of the lowest form. I urge you, fall to; if you can deride an onion, you can devour an onion.
FORD. Hold, hold; you have humbled him sufficiently, sir.
EVANS. I say I will see him eat the better portion of this onion, or I will beat his skull for a fortnight together. Chew, I pray you; it is sovereign remedy for your fresh wound and your bloody foolish head.
BARDOLPH. Must I chew?
EVANS. Yes, certainly and without doubt or disputation or any manner of ambiguity whatsoever.
BARDOLPH. By this onion, I will have a terrible and prolonged revenge. I eat, I eat, I do swear it—
EVANS. Eat, I entreat you. Will you have somewhat more to accompany your onion? There is not onion enough here to swear upon credibly.
BARDOLPH. Stay thy stick; thou seest plainly that I eat.
EVANS. Much good may it do you, scurvy knave, and heartily so. Nay, waste nothing; the outer skin is wholesome for your cracked pate. When hereafter you encounter onions in your travels, I pray you show them all reverence. That is the sum of my instruction.
BARDOLPH. Agreed.
EVANS. Ay, onions are good. Here, take this coin toward the mending of your head.
BARDOLPH. A coin! To me!
EVANS. Yes, truly and in honest faith you will accept it; else I have a second onion about my person which you shall likewise eat.
BARDOLPH. I take this coin as earnest pledge of my revenge.
EVANS. If I stand in your debt, I will discharge it in blows. You shall deal in timber, and purchase nothing from me but cudgels. God be with you, and preserve you, and restore your broken skull. [Exit.]
BARDOLPH. The very stars shall hear of this outrage.
FORD. Go, go; you are a dissembling and cowardly rascal. Will you make sport of an ancient and honourable custom, observed out of reverence and carried as a worthy emblem of valour long past, and yet not venture to make good in your actions one syllable of your boasting? I have witnessed you sneering and jeering at this gentleman on no fewer than three occasions. You supposed that because he could not command the English tongue with native ease, he was therefore unable to command an English stick. You have found your error; and let a Welsh lesson henceforth teach you what an English gentleman ought to be. Fare you well. [Exit.]
BARDOLPH. Does Fortune make a fool of me at last? Word comes to me that Meg is dead in the hospital, carried off by the pox; and my last refuge is utterly destroyed. I grow old, and from these weary bones all dignity has been beaten out. Well, I shall turn to roguery, and lend my hand to picking pockets on the road. To England will I creep, and there commit my petty thefts; and I shall patch these beaten scars and swear before all men that I earned them fighting on the fields of France. [Exit.]

He remembered someone had suggested they hand him over to the authorities at once. He could not bring back the face of whoever had said it, and even now he did not know who it was, but he remembered turning and making some reply.... So that was the explanation of last night's terror. The most appalling thought was that he had very nearly destroyed himself — had come within a breath of ruin — over something so utterly insignificant. So this man could report nothing beyond his asking about the apartment and the traces of blood. So Ilya Petrovitch likewise had nothing but that delirium, no real evidence, only this psychology which proves nothing either way, nothing definite. And if no further facts emerged (and they must not, they absolutely must not!) then... then what could they do to him? How could they convict him, even supposing they arrested him?
And Ilya Petrovitch had only just now learned about the apartment and had not known of it before.
"Was it you who told him... that I had been there?" he asked suddenly, struck by a thought that came on him all at once.
"Told who?"
"The examining officer. The one in charge of the inquiry."
"Yes. The yard-keepers would not go themselves, so I went alone."
"Today?"
"I arrived two minutes before you did. And I was there — I heard everything, how he pressed you."
"Where? How? When?"
"In the adjoining room. I sat there the whole time."
"What? Then it was you — the surprise he had prepared? But how is such a thing possible? Good God!"
"I could see the yard-keepers had no intention of doing as I asked," the man began; "too late now, they said, and he may take it badly that we did not come sooner. I was troubled by it and could not sleep, and so I began to make my own inquiries. Yesterday I found out where I needed to go, and this morning I went. The first time he was not there. An hour later he could not receive me. I came a third time and they let me through. I told him everything, exactly as it had happened, and he began pacing the room and striking himself on the chest. 'What do you mean by this negligence? Had I been told sooner, I would have taken him then and there!' Then he ran to the door, called someone, spoke with him in a low voice in the corner, and then turned back to me — questioning me, reproaching me. He reproached me at considerable length. I told him everything, and told him too that you had not ventured a single word in answer to me the day before, and that you had not known me. He began pacing again, striking himself on the chest, working himself into a fury, back and forth across the room — and when they announced your arrival he sent me into the next room. 'Wait there,' he said. 'Sit still and do not stir, whatever you may hear.' He placed a chair for me himself and locked the door. 'I may have need of you,' he said. And after Nikolay had been brought in, he let me out as soon as you had gone. 'I will call for you again,' he told me."
"And did he speak with Nikolay while you were still there?"
"He sent me out beforehand, as he had sent you out, before he turned to Nikolay."
The man stood without moving, then bent down suddenly once more, touching the floor with his fingertips.
"Forgive me for the evil I thought of you, and for the wrong I did you with my words."
"God will forgive it," said Mikolayev. And as he spoke, the man bowed once more — not so deeply this time — then turned without haste and left the room.
"It proves nothing either way, nothing either way," Mikolayev repeated to himself, and he stepped out into the street with more certainty than he had felt in days. "Now we shall see who prevails," he said under his breath, a cold and bitter smile crossing his face as he descended the stairs. The bitterness was directed entirely at himself; he recalled his moment of near-collapse with something close to contempt.

PART V
CHAPTER I
The morning after the disastrous scene with Varya and her mother worked a certain sobering effect on Andrei Filippovich. Disagreeable as it was to face, he was obliged, gradually and against every inclination, to accept as a settled and unalterable fact what had seemed to him only the evening before an absurd impossibility. The wounded serpent of his vanity had been moving through him all night, slow and persistent. Rising from his bed, Andrei Filippovich went immediately to the mirror. He was half afraid of what he might find there — that his face might have yellowed in the night, that the night's damage might show in his skin.

He remembered someone had proposed handing him over to the authorities on the spot. He couldn't recall the face — couldn't place it even now — but he remembered turning and saying something in reply.... So that was what lay behind last night's terror. The most appalling part was that he had nearly ruined himself, come within a hair of destruction, over something so completely trivial. This man could tell them nothing except that he had asked about the apartment and the bloodstains. Ilya Petrovitch had nothing beyond that delirium, no real evidence, only this psychology that cuts both ways — nothing solid, nothing certain. And if no further facts came out (they must not, they simply must not!) — then what? What could they do to him? How could they convict him, even if they arrested him?
Besides, Ilya Petrovitch had only just learned about the apartment. He hadn't known before today.
"Was it you who told him... that I had been there?" he asked, a sudden thought seizing him.
"Told who?"
"The examining officer. The one running the inquiry."
"Yes. The yard-keepers wouldn't go, so I went myself."
"Today?"
"Two minutes before you arrived. I heard everything — how he went at you."
"Where? How is that possible?"
"The next room. I sat there the whole time."
"You were the surprise, then? But how — good God, how did it come to this?"
"The yard-keepers made it plain they wouldn't help," the man began. "Too late now, they said, and besides he might be angry we hadn't come sooner. It troubled me. I couldn't sleep. I started making inquiries myself, found out yesterday where to go, and came this morning. First visit he wasn't in. Second visit he couldn't see me. Third time they showed me through. I told him everything, just as it happened — and he started pacing the room, beating himself on the chest. 'What do you mean by this? If I had known, I would have arrested him on the spot!' Then he ran out, pulled someone into the corner, talked in a low voice, came back to me and started in with questions and reproaches. Scolded me thoroughly. I told him everything — told him too that you hadn't said a word back to me yesterday, hadn't even recognised me. He was off again then, pacing and striking himself on the chest. And when you were announced, he sent me into the next room. 'Sit there,' he said. 'Don't move, whatever you hear.' He locked the door himself. 'I may want you,' he said. Then after Nikolay was brought he let me out as soon as you'd gone. 'I'll send for you again,' he told me."
"And Nikolay — did he question him while you were still there?"
"He got rid of me first, same as you. Before he said a word to Nikolay."
The man stood motionless a moment, then bent down abruptly and touched the floor with his fingers.
"Forgive me — the evil thoughts I had, and the slander."
"God will forgive you," said Mikolayev. The man bowed once more, not so low this time, then turned and quietly left.
"Cuts both ways — all of it cuts both ways now," Mikolayev kept repeating, and he stepped out into the street steadier than he had felt in days. "Now we'll fight," he said, a cold smile on his lips as he went down the stairs. The coldness was for himself; he thought back on his near-collapse with something that felt very much like contempt.

PART V
CHAPTER I
The morning after that disastrous scene with Varya and her mother brought a certain unwelcome clarity with it. Hard as it was to swallow, Andrei Filippovich found himself forced — slowly, stubbornly, against every instinct — to accept as finished and irreversible what had struck him only yesterday as impossible. The snake of wounded pride had been at him all night. Getting out of bed, he went straight to the mirror. He was almost afraid to look — half-convinced, in some obscure way, that the night had yellowed his skin.

"One can't be expected to copy documents without a decent pen. And the ink they give us is barely fit for pigs."
"Don't quarrel with your bread and butter."
"What bread and butter? They pay next to nothing for copying. What good is it?" he answered, reluctantly, as though the words were meant more for himself than for her.
"And I suppose you want to be rich all at once?"
He looked at her in an odd way. "Yes," he said, after a short pause, with unexpected firmness. "I do."
"Well, don't look at me like that, you'll frighten me to death. Do you want me to bring up the soup or not?"
"As you like."
"Oh — I nearly forgot. Something came for you this morning while you were sleeping. A letter."
"A letter? For me? From whom?"
"How should I know. I paid two copecks to the boy who brought it, out of my own pocket. You'll give it back?"
"Bring it here, for heaven's sake — bring it at once!" cried Volkov, suddenly agitated. "Bring it now!"
A moment later she placed it in his hands. He saw at once what it was: a letter from his sister, from the town of K——. He felt the colour leave his face. It had been a very long time. But beyond the simple shock of it, something else moved in him too, something he could not immediately name.
"Fedosya, go — please — here are your two copecks, but go, go quickly, please."
The letter trembled in his fingers. He could not bring himself to open it while she was still in the room; he needed to be entirely alone with it. When at last her shuffling steps had faded down the corridor, he raised the letter to his lips and held it there for a moment. Then he turned it over and studied the address — the careful, upright hand of his sister, each letter formed with that particular deliberateness he had known since childhood. He hesitated. Some obscure dread seemed to hold him back. Then he broke the seal.
It was a substantial letter, three sheets covered on both sides in a close and careful hand.
"My dear Aleksei," his sister had written, "it is nearly three months since I last wrote to you, and I know that must have caused you worry, though I hope you will not think too harshly of me for my silence once you have read everything I have to tell you. You know I think of you constantly. You and mother are everything to me — my one certainty in all this uncertainty.
"I was very sorry to learn, through mother's last letter to me, that you have had to abandon your position at the office and that the private tutoring has also come to nothing. I cannot tell you how it grieved me. My own situation here has not permitted me to send you anything, though God knows I have wished to. I earn thirty roubles a year in this household, of which I was advanced twenty when I first arrived, and as you will understand, I have been paying that back a little each month and am only now nearly clear of it. The Starovoitovs are not unkind people, on the whole, but they are careful with money as only provincial merchants know how to be, and there is little room to ask for more than what was agreed.
"But now, dear Aleksei, I have something to tell you which I hope will be, at least in part, good news. I am not to remain here much longer. The arrangement is coming to an end at Michaelmas, and I find I am not sorry for it. There are things I have not told you — things I did not want to put into a letter, not knowing whose hands it might pass through before reaching you — but I will tell you everything now, plainly and in order, so that you may understand what the past months have truly been like and why I kept silent about them for so long.
"When you wrote in the spring asking whether all was well, and saying you had heard certain things from Petrov — I scarcely knew how to answer you. Had I told you the truth, I am quite sure you would have done something rash. I know your nature, Alyosha. You would not have stayed where you were. And what would have become of your own situation then? I could not be the cause of that. So I told you only that things were tolerable, which was true enough, up to a point.
"But now I can tell you everything."
