(CNN) The 2023 college basketball season has delivered everything fans could have hoped for: packed arenas, deafening student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that remind you why March became its own cultural institution.
All of that will be on full display when two of the sport's historic powerhouses collide in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden in New York City — the self-proclaimed mecca of basketball. It's top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette, and the stakes could hardly be higher.
The Husky Machine
UConn has long been one of college basketball's most decorated programs. The Huskies have won five national championships and have become synonymous with tournament excellence under coach Dan Hurley, who has steadily rebuilt the program into a genuine superpower since arriving in Storrs in 2018.
This season, UConn entered the year as the clear favorite after dismantling the field en route to a national title the previous spring. Their roster turned over significantly, losing four players to the NBA Draft, but Hurley simply reloaded. That has become something of a trademark.
The Huskies opened the year 7-0 before a tense road trip to Villanova nearly derailed things. Trailing by nine with under four minutes to play, freshman guard Solo Ball — who had struggled to find his footing early in the season — took over, scoring eight straight points and setting up the game-winning layup with eleven seconds left. UConn survived, 74-73.
They have not lost since. A 17-1 conference record heading into Championship weekend is as dominant a run as the Big East has seen in years, and Ball has gone from question mark to the frontrunner for Big East Player of the Year. His emergence has given UConn something even their previous title team lacked: a genuine go-to scorer in the closing minutes of tight games.
"He's grown up before our eyes," Hurley told reporters after UConn's regular season finale. "Early on, he was trying to do too much. Now he trusts the system, trusts his teammates, and when the moment gets big, he rises."
The Golden Eagles Circle
Marquette's path to this moment has been equally compelling, if a little more turbulent. Head coach Shaka Smart, now in his third season in Milwaukee, finally has the pieces to make a genuine title run — and his team knows it.
The Golden Eagles opened the year unranked but won eleven of their first thirteen games, climbing steadily into the top ten. Their calling card has been suffocating perimeter defense and a balanced offense that features five players averaging double figures — the first Marquette team to accomplish that in over two decades.
Their only real stumble came in late January, a two-game losing skid against Providence and Georgetown that briefly threatened to derail their conference title hopes. Smart responded by simplifying the offense and shifting to a smaller, quicker lineup — a gamble that paid off immediately. Marquette won eight straight to close the regular season, including an emphatic 81-64 road win over then-No. 6 Creighton.
Senior guard Kam Jones has been the engine of it all, averaging 22 points per game since the lineup change and drawing comparisons to previous Marquette greats. "We went through a tough stretch and it made us better," Jones said after the regular season ended. "We know who we are now. We stopped trying to be perfect and started playing together."
What's at stake?
For UConn, a Big East title would be the program's sixth in the Dan Hurley era and would all but cement their status as the No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament. More than that, a win would validate their claim to being the best team in the country — a claim a few voters still dispute despite their record.
For Marquette, the calculus is different. Smart is 0-4 against UConn since arriving at the program, and the Golden Eagles have not won the Big East Tournament since 2003. A win on Saturday would shatter both of those storylines at once, and with Selection Sunday one day away, it would send the program into the tournament on the kind of momentum that has historically translated to deep runs.
Two years ago, a different Marquette team reached the Elite Eight before bowing out to eventual champion Kansas. Smart and his players believe this group is better. Whether they're right depends on 40 minutes in the Garden.
"We've put ourselves in a position to compete for something real," Smart said this week. "Now we have to go do it. Nobody's going to hand us anything — especially not them."

(CNN) Should Saturday's NBA playoff game be broadcast on ESPN or framed and hung in the Smithsonian?
That's the question swirling around LeBron James and Kevin Durant ahead of their second-round clash this weekend — the first postseason series in NBA history to feature two opposing players each with more than 27,000 career regular-season points still actively competing at an elite level.
James, 40, posted a characteristically understated message on Instagram ahead of the series: a black-and-white photo of the two men shaking hands at midcourt, captioned simply "Legacy." Durant, 36, responded within the hour: "Let's give them something to remember."
The combined career statistics these two men have accumulated defy easy summary. Between them: eight NBA championships, six scoring titles, two Olympic gold medals apiece, and more All-NBA selections than any two players in the league's modern era. Both rank in the all-time top five in points scored. Historians of the sport have spent the better part of a decade arguing over which of them deserves to be called the most skilled offensive player the game has ever produced.
Older, not slower
They may not have the runway that younger stars like Jayson Tatum, Anthony Edwards, or Shai Gilgeous-Alexander still enjoy, but dismissing either man as a spent force would be a serious mistake.
James, completing his 22nd season after joining the Golden State Warriors on a one-year deal that raised eyebrows across the league last summer, has averaged 24 points, nine assists, and seven rebounds per game in the regular season — numbers that would be remarkable for a player a decade younger. Critics questioned whether he could adapt to a new system under head coach Steve Kerr. The answer has been emphatic.
The Warriors and Durant's Phoenix Suns met twice during the regular season. Phoenix won both, including a dominant 118-97 victory in February in which Durant scored 38 points and held James to 19 on nine-of-twenty-two shooting. James has not forgotten.
"That one stung," he admitted to reporters on Thursday. "But we're a different team now than we were in February. I'm a different player. We've had time to build something here and I think people are starting to see what that looks like."
Durant, meanwhile, is fresh off dismantling a young Oklahoma City Thunder team in the first round, averaging 31 points per game in a five-game series that was closer than the outcome suggested. Head coach Frank Vogel has had five seasons to build an offense around Durant's singular abilities, and the results have been quietly remarkable: three Western Conference Finals appearances and one championship in 2022.
"This is where you want to be," Durant told reporters after closing out the Thunder. "This matchup, this stage. I've been thinking about it since the bracket came out."
What's at stake?
For James, the calculus is straightforward. A deep playoff run — ideally a championship — with a third different franchise would cement an argument many already believe is settled: that he is the greatest to ever play the game. A title with Golden State, following rings with Cleveland and the Los Angeles Lakers, would be unprecedented.
For Durant, the stakes are no less significant. At 36, he is widely expected to announce his retirement at season's end regardless of outcome. A second championship, won against the player who has defined his era, would be the crowning note to a career that needs no further justification — but would certainly benefit from one.
The two men have faced each other in the playoffs three times previously, with Durant's teams holding a 2-1 series advantage. Each meeting has produced at least one moment that replayed for years afterward on highlight reels and in arguments between fans.
"I've known for a long time that this is what the end of my career would look like," Durant said on Thursday. "Competing at the highest level, against the best. That's all I've ever wanted. Sunday gives us that."

(CNN) It was marketed as an unbreakable fortress for sensitive corporate communications — a premium encrypted messaging platform promising total privacy from competitors, hackers, and governments alike. Instead, it became one of the most productive surveillance tools European law enforcement had ever deployed against organized crime.
Authorities in the Netherlands, France, and Belgium revealed on Monday that they had secretly infiltrated EncroChat, an encrypted communications network widely used by criminal networks across Europe, for nearly two years — harvesting tens of millions of messages and triggering one of the continent's largest coordinated law enforcement operations in recent memory.
The joint operation, codenamed "Operation Emma," was led by Europol and Eurojust in coordination with national police forces across more than a dozen countries. Officials said the infiltration of the network had produced over 115 million intercepted messages, leading to more than 1,500 arrests across Europe, the seizure of over 30 metric tons of narcotics, and the confiscation of more than 180 million euros in cash and assets.
Speaking at a press conference in The Hague on Monday, Europol Executive Director Catherine De Bolle described the operation as a landmark moment for cross-border law enforcement. "For the first time, we were able to read the real-time communications of criminal organizations at scale," she said. "These were not low-level offenders. These were the highest levels of organized crime — the planners, the financiers, the decision-makers."
EncroChat devices, which sold for several thousand euros each on the black market, were modified smartphones with the camera, microphone and GPS functions disabled. They could only communicate with other EncroChat devices and were marketed explicitly to users who required, in the words of the platform's own promotional material, "total operational security." The platform had an estimated 60,000 users worldwide at its peak, the majority of whom, authorities allege, were engaged in criminal activity.
The breakthrough came when French cyber-investigators managed to implant malware onto the EncroChat network's servers, located in France, allowing them to intercept messages before they were encrypted. The operation ran covertly for several months before EncroChat administrators detected the intrusion and shut the platform down in June 2020, sending a warning to users telling them to destroy their handsets immediately.
By then, authorities say, the damage — from the criminals' perspective — was done. The intercepted communications contained detailed discussions of drug shipments, weapons deals, contract killings, and money laundering operations. In several cases, law enforcement agencies were able to intervene before planned attacks took place, including the disruption of at least three alleged contract murders in the Netherlands alone.
Not all of the operation's legal foundations have gone uncontested. Defense lawyers in multiple countries have challenged the admissibility of EncroChat evidence in court, arguing that the mass interception of private communications violated the rights of defendants. Several cases in Germany and the United Kingdom are still working through the courts, with judges reaching differing conclusions on the legality of how the data was obtained and shared between jurisdictions.
Europol said Monday that further arrests were anticipated as investigators continued to work through the volume of material collected, and that related operations targeting successor networks were already underway.
"Criminal networks will always look for new tools," De Bolle said. "But this operation has shown that no platform is beyond reach."

(CNN) A prominent Premier League manager and members of his coaching staff have been targeted with threatening messages on social media following a contentious tactical decision that contributed to his club's defeat in a high-profile match over the weekend.
Brentford manager Thomas Frank confirmed on Monday that he and several of his backroom staff had received abusive messages, including threats of physical harm, after his decision to substitute a key forward in the second half was widely blamed by supporters for costing the team a crucial victory. Frank said he had reported the messages to the police and had been in contact with the Premier League.
"It is deeply upsetting, not just for me but for my family and for members of my staff who have nothing to do with decisions made on the pitch," Frank said in a statement released through the club. "I have been in football long enough to know that fans are passionate and that results matter. But there is a line, and threats of violence are far beyond it."
The Premier League condemned the abuse in firm terms. "Threats of violence and intimidatory behavior directed at any manager, player, official, or member of staff are completely unacceptable," said a spokesperson. "We fully support Thomas and his staff in reporting this matter to police and will cooperate fully with any investigation."
The Professional Footballers' Association and the League Managers Association issued a joint statement expressing solidarity with Frank and calling for urgent action from social media companies. "This is not an isolated incident. Managers, players and officials are routinely subjected to abuse that in any other professional context would be treated as a serious criminal matter," the statement read. "Social media platforms must do more — proactively and immediately — to identify and remove threatening content and share information with law enforcement."
The Metropolitan Police confirmed they had received a report and that enquiries were ongoing. "We take all reports of malicious communications seriously," a spokesperson said. "Threatening behavior online is not consequence-free, and those found to have committed offenses will be pursued."
Frank is expected to take charge of Brentford's next fixture on Thursday, though the club said it was in ongoing discussions with the Premier League about security arrangements. He briefly addressed reporters ahead of the match, striking a composed but clearly frustrated tone.
"I love this game. I love managing in this league," he said. "But it has to be safe for everyone involved — not just the players on the pitch, but the people on the touchline, in the offices, and at home."
Several current Premier League players took to social media to express support for Frank, with a number calling on platforms to take greater responsibility for content posted on their sites. Britain's Sports Minister also weighed in, saying the government's forthcoming Online Safety legislation would place new legal obligations on platforms to act swiftly against criminal abuse.

Brussels, Belgium
Flemish nationalist parties secured enough seats on Sunday in Belgium's regional parliament to extend their majority, though a resilient performance by Flemish social democrats suggested that coalition negotiations in Brussels could prove long and complicated.
With over 98% of ballots counted, the combined nationalist bloc took 52.3% of the vote, their strongest showing in a decade. The most probable outcome was a continuation of the existing center-right Flemish coalition, though smaller parties held significant leverage over the final shape of any government.
The result is unlikely to trigger any immediate push for formal secession from Belgium. While tensions between Dutch-speaking Flanders and French-speaking Wallonia remain a persistent feature of Belgian political life, most voters on Sunday cited economic anxieties and the rising cost of living as their primary concerns — not the constitutional question of the country's future.
Turnout was 68%, down from 74% at the previous regional election, a dip that analysts said may have modestly benefited nationalist parties whose core supporters tend to vote at higher rates in lower-participation elections.
The centre-right New Flemish Alliance, known by its Flemish initials N-VA, remained the dominant force in the region, winning an estimated 29 seats in the 124-seat Flemish Parliament. Party leader Bart De Wever declared the result a mandate for greater autonomy and renewed fiscal transfers between the regions.
"Flanders has spoken clearly," De Wever told supporters at the party's election night gathering in Ghent. "We want control over our own future — our own budget, our own social policy, our own choices. That conversation with Brussels cannot be delayed any longer."
The far-right Vlaams Belang, which has increasingly competed with N-VA for the Flemish nationalist vote in recent cycles, won an estimated 26 seats, a slight decline from its previous high-water mark. The party's leader, Tom Van Grieken, nonetheless framed the result as a continued rejection of what he called Belgium's "dysfunctional federal model."
The Flemish social democrats, Vooruit, delivered the evening's most notable surprise, winning 21 seats — a gain of four — and positioning themselves as a potentially decisive partner in any coalition talks. Party leader Melissa Depraeter said her strong showing showed that Flemish voters wanted economic reform, not constitutional upheaval.
"People are worried about their energy bills, their wages, their children's futures," Depraeter told reporters as results came in. "They did not vote tonight for an abstract debate about borders. They voted for change."
For Belgian Prime Minister Georges-Louis Bouchez, whose MR party governs at the federal level in an awkward coalition that spans the linguistic divide, the result offered mixed signals. N-VA's continued dominance deepens pressure on the federal government to accelerate talks on a new state reform, a process that has stalled repeatedly in the current legislative term.
Constitutional experts noted that the path to any formal partition of Belgium — a scenario that resurfaces in political discussion every few years — remains extraordinarily complicated, requiring approval across multiple parliaments and a level of cross-community consensus that does not currently exist.
For now, the most immediate question is a simpler one: who will govern Flanders, and on what terms.

Brussels, Belgium
Flemish nationalist parties won enough seats on Sunday to extend their majority in Belgium's regional parliament, though a stronger-than-expected performance from Flemish social democrats signaled that the road to a new coalition government in Brussels would be anything but smooth.
With nearly 99% of ballots counted, the combined nationalist bloc claimed 52.3% of the vote — their strongest result in a decade. A continuation of the existing center-right Flemish coalition appeared the most likely outcome, though smaller parties retained enough leverage to complicate negotiations significantly.
None of this is likely to produce any immediate push toward formal secession. The question of Belgium's constitutional future — the long-running tension between Dutch-speaking Flanders and French-speaking Wallonia — remains live, but voters on Sunday were more preoccupied with energy prices and the cost of living than with the country's survival as a single state.
Turnout came in at 68%, down from 74% in the previous regional election. Analysts suggested the lower figure may have modestly favored nationalist parties, whose supporters tend to show up in greater numbers when broader enthusiasm fades.
The centre-right New Flemish Alliance, or N-VA, held its position as the region's dominant political force, winning an estimated 29 seats in the 124-seat Flemish Parliament. Party leader Bart De Wever framed the result as a mandate for deeper autonomy and a renegotiation of fiscal arrangements between the regions.
"Flanders has spoken clearly," De Wever told supporters in Ghent on Sunday night. "We want control over our own future — our own budget, our own social policy. That conversation cannot be delayed any longer."
The far-right Vlaams Belang, which has spent several election cycles eating into N-VA's nationalist base, won around 26 seats — a modest decline from its previous peak. Leader Tom Van Grieken nonetheless insisted the result was a further rebuke of what he called Belgium's "dysfunctional federal model."
The evening's real story, though, may have been Vooruit. The Flemish social democrats picked up four seats to reach 21, instantly positioning themselves as a potentially pivotal player in coalition talks. Leader Melissa Depraeter was blunt about what she thought Sunday's vote actually meant.
"People are worried about their energy bills, their wages, their children's futures," she told reporters as results came in. "They did not vote tonight for an abstract debate about borders."
For federal Prime Minister Georges-Louis Bouchez, the results were mixed at best. N-VA's dominance deepens pressure on his already-strained coalition to accelerate stalled talks on a new state reform — a process that has collapsed repeatedly over the past several years.
Constitutional experts were quick to note, as they tend to after every Flemish election, that the formal partition of Belgium would require approval across multiple parliaments and a level of cross-community consensus that remains nowhere in sight.
For now, the question is more prosaic: who governs Flanders, and what they will have to give away to do it.

(CNN) After years of anticipation and months of mounting uncertainty, the Paris 2024 Summer Olympics are set to open in spectacular fashion along the banks of the Seine — but the road to Friday's ceremony has been anything but smooth.
Competitive events began Tuesday with football and rugby sevens matches held across multiple cities, as is customary before the formal opening. But overshadowing the early action has been a wave of security concerns, logistical strain, and persistent questions about whether one of the world's most densely populated capitals can absorb the demands of hosting the planet's largest sporting event.
French authorities have deployed approximately 45,000 police officers and 10,000 military personnel across Paris in what officials are calling the most extensive security operation in the country's history. The unprecedented scale of Friday's opening ceremony — which will take place outdoors along a six-kilometer stretch of the Seine River rather than inside a stadium — has drawn particular scrutiny, with an estimated 300,000 ticket holders and up to 222 delegations of athletes expected to participate in the floating parade.
"The scale of what we are attempting has no precedent in Olympic history," Paris 2024 chief organizer Tony Estanguet acknowledged at a press conference on Monday. "There are risks. We are managing them seriously and with every resource available to us."
Those reassurances have not fully calmed nerves. Just days before the ceremony, French authorities arrested several individuals in connection with an alleged plot to disrupt the Games, prompting an emergency security review. The French interior ministry confirmed the arrests but declined to provide further details, citing the ongoing nature of the investigation.
Transport networks have also come under pressure. Paris's metro system, already operating near capacity during peak summer tourism season, has struggled to absorb the additional load of arriving athletes, journalists, and spectators. Long queues at Charles de Gaulle airport and widespread delays on regional train lines prompted complaints from delegations arriving over the weekend.
The athletes' village, located in Saint-Denis just north of the city, has itself become a talking point. Several national Olympic committees flagged concerns about the absence of air conditioning in residential units — an unusual omission for a summer Games — with organizers defending the decision on environmental grounds and pointing to distributed cooling units as an alternative. The United States, Britain, Canada, and several other delegations announced they had installed their own cooling systems at their own expense.
American swimmer Caeleb Dressel, who is bidding to add to his five gold medals from Tokyo, was diplomatic when asked. "It's warm," he said with a smile. "We've dealt with it."
Los Angeles looks ahead
With Paris finally underway, attention in Olympic circles has already begun to shift toward 2028, when the Games move to Los Angeles for the third time. The LA28 organizing committee is expected to present a progress report to the International Olympic Committee later this week, with officials describing the city's preparations as on schedule.
Los Angeles previously hosted the Games in 1932 and 1984. The 1984 edition, held during a Soviet-led boycott, is widely remembered as a financial and organizational success that helped rescue the Olympic movement from a period of crisis.
LA28 chairman Casey Wasserman sought to frame the 2028 Games as an opportunity to build on Paris's ambitions. "Every Games teaches the next host something new," he told reporters on the sidelines of a pre-opening reception. "We are watching, learning, and preparing."
The 2032 Summer Olympics are scheduled for Brisbane, Australia, which was confirmed as host city in 2021. It will be the third time Australia has hosted the Summer Games, following Melbourne in 1956 and Sydney in 2000.

(CNN) Los Angeles Dodgers outfielder Cody Bellinger said he was grateful to have walked away unharmed after a serious car accident on a highway outside Phoenix, Arizona, on Saturday, just days before the start of the team's spring training camp in Glendale.
Bellinger told reporters Sunday that he was driving to the Dodgers' training facility when a vehicle travelling at high speed crossed into his lane on Interstate 10 and sideswiped his SUV, sending it into the guardrail. Bellinger said his vehicle sustained significant damage to the driver's side and that he was briefly shaken before realizing he had escaped serious injury.
"It happened so fast," Bellinger said. "One second everything is normal, and then you're just trying to figure out what just happened. I'm really lucky."
The 2019 National League MVP said the incident had given him a fresh sense of perspective heading into the new season. "You get so caught up in the grind, in the preparation, that you forget to step back and appreciate that you're healthy and you're here," he said. "Yesterday reminded me of that pretty quickly."
Bellinger's agent confirmed that the outfielder had been examined by team medical staff on Sunday morning and was cleared of any injuries. He was present and took part in light workouts at the Dodgers' complex on Sunday afternoon, according to a team spokesperson.
The Arizona Department of Public Safety confirmed the incident and said the other driver had been cited for unsafe lane change. No other vehicles were involved and no injuries were reported.
Bellinger, who is entering his third season with the Dodgers after signing a multi-year contract extension last winter, is expected to be in the starting lineup when the team opens their Cactus League schedule next week.
"I called my family right after," Bellinger said. "That was the first thing I did. Just wanted them to know I was OK. Honestly, I'm just happy to be here talking to you guys."

(CNN) A sweeping seven-year investigation into corruption and financial misconduct across international athletics concluded Wednesday, as the US Department of Justice announced it would return $165 million to the World Athletics governing body, the International Association of Athletics Federations, and several national athletics federations identified as victims of a systemic doping-cover-up bribery scheme.
The DOJ determined that officials within multiple organizations had accepted payments from athletes and their coaches in exchange for suppressing positive doping test results, allowing competitors to continue competing at the highest levels of the sport — including at the Olympic Games — while concealing banned substance use from anti-doping authorities.
"This was a profound betrayal of clean athletes everywhere," said World Athletics President Sebastian Coe. "Money that was stolen from the integrity of our sport will now be returned to programs that protect it. This is a significant day, but it is also a reminder of how much damage was done and how much work remains."
The investigation, which began in 2016 following a series of whistleblower disclosures and an explosive report by an independent commission established by the World Anti-Doping Agency, resulted in charges against 38 individuals and four corporate entities across 15 countries. Among those charged were former senior officials of the Russian Athletics Federation, intermediaries operating in multiple jurisdictions, and representatives of several sports management agencies.
Charges included wire fraud, money laundering, obstruction of justice, and conspiracy to defraud anti-doping bodies. Several individuals have already pleaded guilty, while others are still awaiting trial. The DOJ said the investigation remained ongoing.
The recovered funds will be channeled into a newly established Clean Sport Development Fund, to be jointly administered by World Athletics and an independent oversight board. According to the DOJ, the fund will support anti-doping education initiatives, athlete welfare programs, and the development of athletics infrastructure in lower-income countries where governance oversight has historically been weakest.
"We are committed to making sure this money reaches the athletes and communities it was always meant to serve," Coe said.
The case drew comparisons to the 2015 FIFA corruption scandal, in which US federal prosecutors similarly used wire fraud and money laundering statutes to pursue misconduct in an international sports organization, given that much of the relevant financial activity passed through American banks.
CNN's Washington bureau contributed to this report.

Rome (CNN) Pope Francis will not travel to Lisbon next week for the closing ceremony of World Youth Day, according to a Vatican spokesperson. The announcement follows a period of reduced public engagements for the 86-year-old pontiff, who has been managing ongoing knee and hip problems that have increasingly limited his mobility in recent months.
The Vatican had confirmed earlier on Monday that the Pope would "reluctantly" withdraw from the planned in-person appearance at the Parque Eduardo VII gathering, which is expected to draw hundreds of thousands of young Catholics from around the world. He would instead deliver a blessing and address via video link from the Apostolic Palace.
"The Holy Father deeply regrets that he is unable to be present in Lisbon for this historic occasion but looks forward to being with the faithful in spirit and through his message," the statement read.
Earlier this month, Francis had used particularly blunt language about the church's responsibility to young people during a general audience in St. Peter's Square, drawing applause from pilgrims when he said institutional "timidity" in the face of social injustice was "simply not acceptable."
A senior Vatican official confirmed to CNN that the decision to withdraw from Lisbon was made purely on medical advice and was unrelated to any acute health event. The Pope had been seen using a wheelchair at several recent public engagements, a visible sign of the mobility difficulties he has experienced since undergoing colon surgery in 2021.
Despite the travel cancellation, Francis has continued to carry out a full schedule of meetings and audiences at the Vatican. CNN's review of his public calendar shows he conducted more than fourteen formal engagements in the first three weeks of July alone, including audiences with several heads of state and a lengthy address to a symposium on environmental theology.
He declined to reduce that pace last week, presiding over his regular Wednesday general audience and meeting privately with a delegation of Ukrainian Catholic bishops, despite calls from some within the Curia for a more cautious approach to his schedule.
Last month, the Pope dismissed suggestions he was considering retirement, saying he had "no intention" of following the path taken by his predecessor Benedict XVI. "When the Lord wants me to stop, he will let me know," he told a group of Jesuit journalists in a wide-ranging interview.
Still, the decision not to make the journey to Lisbon — a trip he had publicly expressed enthusiasm for as recently as last month — is the latest in a series of adjustments to his public role. Long-haul foreign travel has become increasingly rare, with shorter regional trips and domestic engagements now making up the bulk of his schedule outside of the Vatican.

(CNN) "There are days when it still doesn't feel real," Dwyane Wade said in a quiet moment after a charity event in Miami last weekend, marking the one-year anniversary of the death of his closest friend in basketball.
One year after the passing of David Stern — the NBA commissioner who transformed the league into a global phenomenon — those whose careers he shaped most directly are still processing a loss that cut deeper than professional admiration. For Wade, and for many others across the league, honoring Stern's legacy has become part of how they carry him forward.
Stern died on January 1, 2020, following a sudden brain hemorrhage. He was 77. Over 30 years as commissioner, he oversaw the NBA's expansion from a domestically struggling league into one of the most recognized sports properties in the world, building platforms for the very players who now mourn him.
"He gave so many of us a stage," Wade said. "Not just to play, but to be who we are. To have opinions, to have a voice. That wasn't always the case and he changed it."
LeBron James, who came of age in the league Stern built and clashed with the commissioner on more than one occasion, spoke about him with unmistakable warmth. "We didn't always see eye to eye and I think he respected that, and I respected him for it," James said. "He was tough, he was honest, and he cared about this league more than anybody."
'He saw the whole board'
Current NBA Commissioner Adam Silver, who served as Stern's deputy for years before succeeding him in 2014, said the past year had only deepened his appreciation for what his predecessor built — and for how much of what the league takes for granted today traces back to decisions Stern made decades ago.
"David had this ability to see around corners," Silver said at a memorial event held in New York last week. "He saw the global game before anyone else did. He saw the business of basketball becoming something that could rival any entertainment property in the world. And then he went and built it."
Silver said he still finds himself reaching for the phone to call Stern when a major decision confronts the league — a reflex that hasn't faded. "I'll probably never fully stop doing that," he admitted.
Among the players who knew Stern personally, the tributes have been consistent in one theme: he treated them as partners, even when the relationship was combative. Chris Paul, president of the National Basketball Players Association, said Stern's willingness to engage directly with players — sometimes fiercely — ultimately made the league stronger.
"He pushed back. Hard, sometimes. But he also listened," Paul said. "That's rarer than people think in this business."
The NBA held a moment of silence before games on New Year's Day to mark the anniversary. Several teams wore commemorative patches on their jerseys, and a tribute video produced by the league's entertainment division aired during halftime of the nationally televised game.
For Silver, the anniversary was a moment to reflect not just on what Stern achieved, but on the standard he set. "Every decision I make, I ask myself whether it moves the game forward," Silver said. "That's what he taught me. That's what he was."

(CNN) The Las Vegas Raiders could become the first NFL team to welcome a full stadium crowd since the pandemic emptied arenas across the country more than a year ago, after Nevada officials signaled they would allow Allegiant Stadium to operate at full capacity beginning with the team's preseason home opener.
The franchise announced Thursday that Allegiant Stadium would be open to its 65,000-seat capacity for the Raiders' first preseason game against the San Francisco 49ers on August 14, pending final confirmation from the Nevada Department of Health. Raiders President Marc Badain said the organization was "thrilled" at the prospect.
"We built one of the most spectacular stadiums in professional sports in large part to give our fans an experience unlike any other," Badain said in a statement. "To finally be able to welcome all of them through the doors is something we have been working toward since the day this building opened."
The announcement coincided with Nevada Governor Steve Sisolak's decision to lift most remaining capacity restrictions on large indoor venues across the state, citing a sustained decline in Covid-19 hospitalizations and an accelerating vaccination rollout. As of Thursday, approximately 39% of Nevada's adult population had been fully vaccinated, according to state health department figures.
Some health officials expressed caution. Dr. Fermin Leguen of the Southern Nevada Health District acknowledged the improving picture but noted that full indoor stadiums presented a different challenge than outdoor venues. "We are encouraged by the trends," he said at a briefing Thursday. "We also ask that residents continue to exercise personal responsibility."
Fans attending the game will still be encouraged, though not required, to wear masks inside the stadium once Nevada's indoor mask mandate expires on June 1. The team said it would maintain enhanced cleaning protocols and increase ventilation throughout the building.
The Raiders' situation is complicated by the franchise's unusual circumstances. Allegiant Stadium, a $1.9 billion domed facility, opened in July 2020 — just as the pandemic was forcing sports leagues to play without spectators. The Raiders played their entire inaugural season in Las Vegas without a single fan in the building, a deeply frustrating experience for a franchise that had relocated from Oakland specifically to build a new identity and fanbase in the Nevada desert.
A limited number of fans were permitted to attend selected home games during the 2020 season at other NFL venues, but the Raiders were among the teams that played before empty seats for the entire year.
The NFL is expected to confirm full stadium attendance for all 32 teams when the 2021 regular season begins in September, though individual state and local regulations may affect some franchises.
Las Vegas Mayor Carolyn Goodman welcomed the announcement, calling it a significant step in the city's economic recovery. "This city runs on hospitality, on spectacle, on bringing people together," she said. "Having a full house at Allegiant Stadium is exactly the signal Las Vegas needs to send to the world right now."
Not all voices were in agreement. Several epidemiologists cautioned that the Delta variant, which had begun emerging in other parts of the world, posed a potential threat to the reopening timeline. "We are in a better place than we were six months ago," said Dr. Brian Labus of the University of Nevada Las Vegas School of Public Health. "That does not mean the pandemic is over."
As of Thursday, 42,000 tickets for the preseason opener had already been sold. Team officials said the remaining inventory would go on sale by the end of the week.

Throughout recent decades, a growing number of talented professionals from developing nations have chosen to pursue careers in wealthier countries, leaving behind their places of origin. This phenomenon, commonly referred to as brain drain, has sparked considerable debate among policymakers and academics alike. Should developing countries take active measures to retain their skilled workforce, or should they encourage free movement? I personally believe that allowing skilled workers to seek opportunities abroad carries significant long-term benefits for all parties involved.
There are several notable advantages associated with permitting skilled professionals to work overseas. To begin with, workers who gain experience in advanced economies often return with valuable knowledge, updated practices, and broader professional networks. These individuals can subsequently contribute to improving local industries, educational institutions, and healthcare systems. Furthermore, many emigrants send remittances back to their home countries, providing financial support to families and stimulating local economies. This influx of foreign currency can be substantial in smaller or lower-income nations, often exceeding official development aid.
Additional considerations reinforce this perspective. Exposure to competitive international environments pushes skilled workers to refine their abilities and adopt global standards of excellence. Upon returning, these professionals tend to introduce improved methodologies and foster innovation within local institutions. Moreover, countries that maintain open pathways for their citizens to work abroad often develop stronger diplomatic and economic ties with host nations, which can facilitate future trade and cooperation agreements.
In conclusion, while concerns about losing talented individuals are understandable, the broader picture suggests that encouraging skilled emigration can yield positive outcomes for developing nations in the long run. Historically, countries that have embraced this openness have often seen greater economic development and stronger international partnerships compared to those that imposed restrictions on their workforce.

Over the past few decades, talented professionals from poorer nations have increasingly left to build careers in wealthier countries. The debate around this trend — often called brain drain — has grown louder among policymakers and academics. Should developing countries try harder to keep their best workers at home, or does free movement actually serve everyone better? My view is that allowing skilled workers to seek opportunities abroad tends to benefit all sides, even if the advantages are not always immediate.
The most obvious benefit is the knowledge workers bring back. Professionals who spend years in advanced economies do not just earn higher wages — they return with updated practices, broader networks, and exposure to systems that simply do not yet exist in their home countries. That kind of knowledge transfer can do more for a local hospital or university than years of government funding alone. On top of that, emigrants routinely send money home. Remittances from overseas workers frequently outstrip official development aid in smaller economies, giving families financial stability and injecting real currency into local markets.
There is more to consider beyond the economic angle. Working in competitive international settings pushes people to sharpen skills they might never have developed otherwise. When they return — and many do — they tend to raise standards around them, sometimes quite dramatically. Countries that keep these pathways open also tend to build stronger diplomatic and trade ties with the nations hosting their citizens, opening doors in other areas entirely.
The fear of permanently losing talented people is real and worth taking seriously. But looking at the longer arc, nations that have remained open to skilled emigration have generally fared better economically and diplomatically than those that tried to lock talent in. The goal should not be to stop people from leaving — it should be to create conditions worth coming back to.

Some people argue that participating in outdoor activities provides greater developmental benefits for children than engaging with video games. This essay agrees with that position and will present several reasons to support it.
To begin with, outdoor activities contribute significantly to children's physical health. When children run, jump, and engage in active play, their bodies develop strength and endurance while stress is naturally reduced. Medical professionals and researchers have long supported the view that regular physical activity is essential for human health. Doctors, for instance, routinely advise patients suffering from lifestyle-related conditions such as obesity or type 2 diabetes to incorporate more movement into their daily routines. Encouraging children to be active outdoors therefore serves as a preventative measure against such illnesses later in life.
In addition, outdoor activities create opportunities for children to develop meaningful social skills. Interacting with peers in a shared physical environment teaches children how to communicate, cooperate, and resolve conflicts. These experiences help shape a child's character and inform how they relate to others throughout life. Playing team sports, for example, instills values such as fairness, perseverance, and respect for others, qualities that are difficult to develop through a screen.
By contrast, excessive engagement with video games carries notable risks. Children who spend large amounts of time gaming may develop unhealthy dependencies on electronic devices. Furthermore, exposure to violent or aggressive content in certain games has been linked in numerous studies to behavioral changes in young players. The cumulative effect of such exposure can be harmful to a child's emotional and psychological development.
Prolonged screen time also poses a direct threat to children's eyesight. Increased rates of vision problems in younger generations have been widely observed and are frequently associated with extended device use. Even when screen time is used for educational purposes, the physical strain on the eyes remains a genuine concern.
In conclusion, outdoor activity is clearly more beneficial to children's overall development than playing video games. It is therefore the responsibility of parents, educators, and communities to actively promote and encourage children's participation in outdoor play.

There is a widely held view that outdoor activities do more for a child's development than video games do. This essay takes the same position, and the following paragraphs explain why.
Physical health is perhaps the most obvious benefit. Running around outside, whether in organized sport or simple free play, builds strength, burns energy, and helps keep stress in check. This is not just common sense — doctors have backed it for decades. Patients with conditions like obesity or type 2 diabetes are regularly told to move more, and the logic applies just as well to children. Habits formed early tend to stick, so an active childhood is one of the better defenses against lifestyle diseases down the road.
There is also a social dimension that outdoor play handles particularly well. When children share a physical space — a playground, a football pitch, a park — they have to deal with each other in real time. They negotiate, argue, make up, and eventually figure out how to get along. Team sports are a useful example: a child learning to lose gracefully or to support a struggling teammate is picking up something that no amount of screen time easily replicates.
Video games, on the other hand, come with real downsides when use becomes excessive. The risk of dependency on devices is well documented, and for younger children especially, it can creep up quickly. Games with violent or aggressive content add another layer of concern — a body of research has consistently found connections between such content and shifts in children's behavior and emotional responses.
Vision is another issue worth raising. It has become almost unremarkable how many young people now wear glasses, and prolonged exposure to screens is a significant factor. Even when a child is using a computer for something productive, the physical strain on the eyes is still present.
All things considered, outdoor activity comes out well ahead of gaming when it comes to children's development. Parents, teachers, and communities share a responsibility here — not to ban screens outright, but to make sure children are spending enough time outside to grow up healthy, socially capable, and physically well.

The internet has become one of the most widely used tools in modern life, fundamentally changing how people communicate, work, and access information. However, its rapid expansion has given rise to a significant debate: should internet use be regulated by authorities, or should individuals be free to use it without restriction? This essay will examine both sides of the argument before presenting a personal viewpoint.
Those who advocate for internet regulation often base their position on concerns about safety and security. The online environment has become a space where criminal activity is increasingly common. Cybercriminals exploit personal data through hacking, scammers deceive users through fraudulent schemes, and extremist groups use online platforms to spread harmful ideologies. Without some form of oversight, these threats are likely to grow unchecked, putting both individuals and society at risk. Proponents of regulation therefore argue that a controlled internet is a safer one.
On the other side of the debate, opponents of internet regulation emphasize the value of free and open access. The internet has transformed the way businesses operate, enabling companies to reach global audiences at a fraction of the cost of traditional methods. Online platforms also allow individuals to express opinions, access education, and participate in democratic processes. Imposing restrictions, critics argue, risks undermining these freedoms and could be misused by governments to suppress legitimate speech or limit access to information.
Nevertheless, the existence of misinformation, cybercrime, and harmful content online makes a compelling case that some degree of regulation is necessary. The key lies in striking the right balance — implementing controls that target genuinely dangerous content without infringing on the rights of ordinary users.
In conclusion, while unrestricted internet access offers clear benefits in terms of freedom and efficiency, the dangers posed by criminal and harmful online activity cannot be ignored. In my view, carefully managed regulation is not only justifiable but essential to ensuring that the internet remains a safe and productive space for all.

Few technologies have reshaped daily life as thoroughly as the internet. It connects people across continents, drives commerce, and gives individuals access to more information than any library could hold. Yet precisely because it is so powerful and so pervasive, it has become the subject of a persistent and often heated debate: should governments regulate what happens online, or is that kind of control an overreach? This essay looks at both positions before offering a personal conclusion.
The case for regulation tends to start with safety. Spend enough time online and the risks become apparent — hackers targeting personal and financial data, elaborate scams designed to separate people from their money, and extremist content engineered to radicalize vulnerable users. These are not minor inconveniences. They cause real harm, and without some form of oversight there is little to stop them from spreading further. For those who support regulation, the argument is straightforward: an unmonitored internet is a dangerous one.
The opposing view is equally understandable. Open internet access has been transformative in ways that are hard to overstate. Businesses can now reach customers around the world without the costs of traditional advertising. Individuals can learn, organize, and speak freely in ways that were simply not possible before. Critics of regulation worry that once governments have the tools to control online content, those tools are easily turned toward silencing dissent or restricting access to inconvenient truths. It is a concern with historical backing.
That said, the sheer volume of harmful material that circulates online — misinformation, criminal activity, content designed to exploit or endanger — makes it difficult to argue that no intervention is warranted. The real challenge is not whether to regulate, but how to do so without doing more damage than good.
On balance, some level of internet regulation seems not just reasonable but necessary. The goal should be targeted controls that address genuine threats while leaving the open, connected nature of the internet broadly intact. Freedom and safety are not mutually exclusive — but finding that balance requires careful thought, not a blanket approach in either direction.

Many countries around the world are fortunate to possess abundant natural resources such as oil, natural gas, and valuable minerals. While some argue that such wealth breeds complacency among citizens, this essay disagrees with that view. Instead, it will argue that economic wealth derived from natural resources has the potential to improve living standards and motivate citizens to contribute to their country's continued growth and development.
To begin with, resource-based economic wealth creates opportunities that directly benefit ordinary citizens. In economies where employment can be difficult to secure, thriving industries built around natural resources generate a wide range of jobs, from technical and engineering roles to administrative and service positions. Rather than making people careless, these opportunities tend to instill a sense of purpose and contribution. Citizens who are employed and financially stable are more likely to invest in their communities and take pride in their country's progress.
Furthermore, economic wealth has the power to transform how a country is perceived on the global stage. Nations that have developed their natural resources effectively have, in many cases, built modern infrastructure, expanded access to education and healthcare, and raised the overall quality of life for their populations. Human ambition plays a significant role in this process. People naturally desire improved living conditions and greater financial security, and this drive encourages both individuals and governments to make productive use of available resources rather than squandering them.
In conclusion, economic wealth rooted in natural resources is far from a cause of national carelessness. On the contrary, it serves as a foundation for development and a better quality of life. When managed well, such wealth motivates citizens and governments alike to strive for continued progress, raising the country's standing both domestically and internationally.

A significant number of countries sit on considerable reserves of oil, gas, and minerals — resources that, when developed, can generate enormous national wealth. Some people take the view that this kind of prosperity makes citizens complacent, with little incentive to work hard or care about their country's future. This essay disagrees. Natural resource wealth, when put to good use, tends to improve lives and give people more reason to invest in where they live, not less.
One of the clearest benefits is employment. Finding stable work is a challenge in many parts of the world, and industries built around natural resources create jobs across a wide range of skill levels — engineering, logistics, administration, and beyond. It is difficult to argue that this makes people careless. If anything, the opposite is true: when people have work and a reliable income, they tend to feel a stronger connection to their country and a greater stake in how it develops. The workforce that powers these industries is also what sustains them, which gives citizens both purpose and influence.
There is also a broader effect on how a country presents itself to the world. Nations that have channeled resource revenues into infrastructure, education, and public services have, in a number of cases, undergone remarkable transformations within a generation or two. Part of what drives this is simply human nature — people want more comfort, more security, more opportunity for themselves and their families. That ambition, rather than being a flaw, is often what pushes countries forward.
To sum up, resource wealth does not make citizens idle. It opens doors, raises expectations, and provides governments with the means to build something lasting. The desire for a better life is a powerful motivator, and in countries rich with natural resources, that desire has frequently translated into genuine national development.

The graph presents data on the unemployment rate among graduates aged 20 to 24 in a European country, broken down by gender, covering the period from 2008 to 2009.
Overall, unemployment among both male and female graduates followed a broadly similar pattern across the two years, with rates generally declining in the middle months before rising sharply toward the end of each year. Male unemployment consistently exceeded female unemployment throughout the entire period.
In January 2008, the unemployment rate for male graduates stood at approximately 10%, while the rate for female graduates was lower, at around 7%. Both figures declined through April and July of that year, suggesting a period of relative improvement in graduate employment. However, this trend reversed by October 2008, when male unemployment rose sharply to approximately 17% and female unemployment climbed to around 12%, representing notable increases for both groups.
A similar pattern emerged in 2009. Between January and around mid-year, male graduate unemployment declined gradually, though it surged again by October 2009, reaching its peak value for the entire period at approximately 23%. Female graduate unemployment proved more volatile during this year. It fell to its lowest recorded point in April 2009, dropping to roughly 3%, before recovering and then rising steeply. By October 2009, the female unemployment rate had climbed to approximately 14%, its highest point across the full two-year period.
In summary, both genders experienced recurring cycles of decline and sharp increase, with male graduates bearing a consistently higher unemployment rate and both groups reaching their highest levels of unemployment in October 2009.

The graph shows unemployment rates among university graduates aged 20 to 24 in a European country, separated by gender, across 2008 and 2009.
The headline pattern for both groups is fairly consistent: rates tended to fall during the middle of each year before climbing sharply toward October. Male unemployment sat above female unemployment for virtually the entire period.
At the start of 2008, around 10% of male graduates were unemployed, compared to roughly 7% of their female counterparts. Both figures eased through the spring and summer months, pointing to some improvement in the job market for young graduates. That improvement did not last. By October 2008, male unemployment had jumped to about 17% and female unemployment to around 12% — a substantial reversal for both.
2009 followed a broadly similar arc, though with some differences in the detail. Male unemployment drifted downward in the first half of the year, only to spike again in October, this time reaching roughly 23% — the highest point recorded across the full two years. The female trend was less predictable. Unemployment among women fell sharply to about 3% in April, its lowest level in the entire dataset, before turning upward again. By October 2009 it had reached approximately 14%, the highest female rate seen across both years.
Taken together, the data suggests a recurring seasonal or cyclical pattern, with conditions for young graduates worsening notably each October. The gap between male and female unemployment narrowed at certain points but never closed, and by the end of the period both groups were significantly worse off than they had been at the start.

Regarding global wealth distribution, North America holds the largest share at approximately 34%, making it the wealthiest region by a considerable margin. Europe follows in second place with around 30%, and Rich Asia-Pacific contributes approximately 24% of global wealth. Together, these three regions account for the vast majority of the world's wealth. The remaining regions contribute relatively little, with India, Africa, China, and Other Asia-Pacific each holding shares of below 5%, with India and Africa recording identical figures.
When it comes to population, the picture is almost entirely reversed. China has the highest population share of any single region at around 24%, followed closely by India and Europe, both of which account for approximately 15% of the global population. Other Asia-Pacific records a similar figure. In sharp contrast, North America and Rich Asia-Pacific, despite their considerable economic dominance, are home to only around 6% and 5% of the global population respectively.

The graph compares regional shares of global wealth and population, placing economic weight alongside demographic size for several regions around the world.
The most striking takeaway is the disconnect between where wealth is concentrated and where people actually live. The regions with the biggest economies are not the ones with the most people — and the reverse holds just as firmly.
On the wealth side, North America leads with roughly 34% of the global total. Europe is close behind at around 30%, and Rich Asia-Pacific accounts for about 24%. Between them, these three regions hold the overwhelming majority of the world's wealth. Everything else is marginal — India, Africa, China, and Other Asia-Pacific each come in below 5%, with India and Africa sitting at the same figure.
Population tells a very different story. China accounts for about 24% of the global population, more than any other region. India and Europe both sit at around 15%, with Other Asia-Pacific in a similar range. North America and Rich Asia-Pacific, for all their economic weight, are home to just 6% and 5% of the world's people respectively.
The contrast is hard to miss. The three wealthiest regions together hold well over 80% of global wealth but represent a comparatively small slice of the global population. Meanwhile, regions like China, India, and Africa, which together account for a large majority of the world's people, hold only a fraction of its wealth. The data makes the scale of global economic inequality difficult to ignore.

There is a common belief that imposing longer prison sentences is the most effective way to reduce crime in society. However, others contend that alternative approaches may prove more successful. This essay fully agrees with the latter view, as lengthy incarceration alone does not address the root causes of criminal behaviour.
It is worth acknowledging that longer prison sentences do carry certain advantages. The prospect of spending many years behind bars may deter some individuals from committing crimes in the first place. Additionally, an extended period of imprisonment gives offenders time to reflect on their actions, which could, in some cases, contribute to genuine rehabilitation.
Despite these benefits, there are significant drawbacks to relying on long sentences as a primary solution. Individuals who spend prolonged periods in prison often struggle to reintegrate into society upon release. Having been isolated from everyday life for years, they may find it difficult to secure employment or rebuild social connections, making them more vulnerable to reoffending. In this way, long sentences may inadvertently contribute to the very problem they are meant to solve.
More fundamentally, crime is frequently driven by social and economic pressures that imprisonment cannot address. Poverty and unemployment are among the strongest predictors of criminal activity. When people are unable to meet their basic needs, some may turn to crime out of desperation. Governments would therefore be better served by implementing policies that stimulate economic growth, reduce unemployment, and provide adequate social support.
Mental and emotional strain is another contributing factor. The pressures of work, financial instability, and family responsibilities can accumulate to a point where individuals feel overwhelmed. Without accessible mental health support or adequate social safety nets, some may resort to criminal behaviour as a means of coping.
In conclusion, while longer prison sentences may offer a limited deterrent effect, they fall short as a comprehensive solution to crime. Addressing the economic and psychological conditions that lead people toward criminal behaviour is likely to be far more effective in the long term.

A widely held view is that handing down longer prison sentences is the best tool societies have for reducing crime. Others push back on this, arguing that different approaches would work better. This essay sides firmly with the latter position — longer sentences may have a role to play, but they cannot do the job alone.
There is something to be said for the deterrence argument. Knowing that a conviction could mean years in prison might give some people pause before breaking the law. Time behind bars also creates space for reflection, and for some offenders, that period of forced removal from ordinary life genuinely prompts change.
But the limitations become clear when you consider what happens at the end of a long sentence. Someone who has spent years cut off from society often emerges without the skills, contacts, or confidence needed to find work and build a stable life. That kind of vulnerability makes reoffending more likely, not less — which undermines the whole purpose of the punishment.
The deeper issue is that crime rarely appears out of nowhere. Economic hardship is one of the most consistent drivers. When unemployment rises and people cannot afford to meet basic needs, some turn to crime not out of moral failing but out of desperation. Policy responses that target poverty and create economic opportunity are likely to do far more to reduce crime than extending sentences ever could.
Stress and mental burden matter too. Juggling work, debt, childcare, and family care takes a real toll, and without adequate support systems, that pressure can push people toward harmful choices. Addressing these pressures through accessible mental health services and stronger social safety nets deserves far more attention in the crime reduction conversation.
Longer sentences are not worthless, but treating them as the primary solution misses the point. Crime is a symptom. Treating the underlying conditions — poverty, unemployment, mental strain — is where the real gains are to be found.

The graphs illustrate how full-time and part-time employment participation in Australia changed between 1973 and 1993, with data broken down by both gender and age group.
Overall, full-time employment declined for both male and female workers over the two decades, while part-time work became increasingly common. Across both genders, the 15 to 19 age group showed the highest levels of part-time employment participation.
Looking at male workers, those in the 25 to 49 age group showed relatively stable patterns, with full-time and part-time participation rates remaining broadly similar throughout the period and showing no dramatic divergence. This suggests that age had a limited influence on the employment status of male workers within this bracket.
For female workers, however, the picture was considerably more dynamic, with age playing a much more significant role in shaping employment patterns. Among female employees in the 15 to 19 age group, full-time participation was high in 1973, approaching 50%, but had fallen sharply to around 20% by 1993. Part-time work among the same group moved in the opposite direction, rising from approximately 8% in 1973 to around 32% in 1993, representing a substantial shift in how younger women engaged with the workforce over the period.
In summary, the data reveals a clear trend away from full-time and toward part-time employment across the two decades, with this shift being especially pronounced among younger female workers. Male employment patterns, by comparison, remained more stable across age groups throughout the same period.

The graphs track participation in full-time and part-time work in Australia from 1973 to 1993, with figures separated by gender and age group.
The broad trend across both genders is a gradual move away from full-time employment and toward part-time work over the two decades. Among younger workers in particular — those aged 15 to 19 — part-time employment was the dominant mode by the end of the period, regardless of gender.
Male workers in the 25 to 49 bracket tell a fairly uneventful story. Full-time and part-time participation rates in this group stayed relatively close together and did not shift dramatically in either direction over the twenty years. Age, within this range at least, does not appear to have been a strong determining factor for male employment status.
The female data is where the more interesting patterns emerge. Age had a much more visible influence on women's employment choices, and the changes over time were sharper. Young women aged 15 to 19 were heavily concentrated in full-time work in 1973, with participation close to 50%. By 1993 that figure had dropped to around 20%. Part-time work among the same group went the other way — up from roughly 8% in 1973 to about 32% in 1993. That is a substantial reversal within a single age cohort over just two decades.
Taken together, the graphs point to a workforce in transition. Part-time employment steadily gained ground across the board, but the scale of that shift varied considerably depending on gender and age. Younger women experienced the most pronounced changes, while male workers in their prime working years remained comparatively consistent in their employment patterns throughout the period.

The chart presents data on the number of male and female workers alongside the number of factories recorded in England and Wales over a fifty-year period, from 1851 to 1901.
Overall, the most notable contrast in the data is between the workforce figures and the factory count. While the number of both male and female workers declined consistently over the period, the number of factories followed an opposing trend, increasing substantially across the same timeframe.
In 1851, male workers numbered 287,100, a figure roughly twice that of female workers, which stood at 190,000. Both categories then declined steadily throughout the period. By 1901, male worker numbers had fallen to 31,000, while female workers had dropped to approximately 30,000. By the end of the period, the gap between male and female worker numbers, which had been considerable at the outset, had narrowed to almost nothing.
The trajectory of factory numbers was markedly different. Starting at just 225 factories in 1851, this figure rose considerably over the following decades, reaching its peak of 721 in 1881 — the highest point recorded across the entire period. Following this peak, the number of factories experienced a modest decline, falling by approximately 40 every decade. Despite this late decrease, the total still stood at 600 by 1901, more than double the figure recorded fifty years earlier.
In summary, the data reflects a significant restructuring of industrial activity in England and Wales during the second half of the nineteenth century, with fewer but larger operations seemingly replacing the more labour-intensive model of earlier decades.

The chart covers a fifty-year snapshot of industrial England and Wales, tracking the number of male and female workers and the number of factories from 1851 to 1901.
The headline finding is a clear divergence between two trends running in opposite directions. Workers — both male and female — became steadily fewer over the period, while the number of factories grew. That combination points toward a shift in how industry was organized rather than a simple decline in activity.
At the start of the period, male workers numbered 287,100, nearly double the female figure of 190,000. From there, both declined without interruption. By 1901, male workers had fallen to 31,000 and female workers to around 30,000 — a dramatic drop for both, and one that effectively erased the gap between them. Two groups that began the period at very different sizes ended it at almost identical levels.
Factories moved in the opposite direction. There were just 225 in 1851, a figure that climbed steadily before peaking at 721 in 1881 — the highest point in the entire dataset. After that, the count dipped slightly, losing roughly 40 factories per decade, and settled at 600 by 1901. So even accounting for the late decline, the number of factories had more than doubled over the fifty years.
The overall picture suggests a period of industrial consolidation. Fewer workers were employed, yet more factories existed — implying that individual operations were becoming larger and more mechanized, absorbing output that had previously required a much greater human workforce.

Male unemployment ran higher than female unemployment at every point in the dataset.
